narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile—including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 31, part-time enrollment status, and low year 1 and year 3 grade deciles of 1—and estimated a 74.5% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar from the outset. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your year 3 grade decile, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your school—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—and signaled to the model a concerning drop or stagnation in academic performance over time. Next in importance was your year 1 grade decile, also at the bottom 10% compared to the average around the 5th or 6th decile, reinforcing early struggles that often predict bar challenges. Your choice of part-time status, uncommon among applicants (only about 7% opt for it versus 93% full-time), further nudged the prediction toward failure, as the model associates it with divided focus that hinders bar preparation for many students. While your undergraduate GPA and LSAT were below averages of 3.21 and 36.7 respectively, they played lesser roles here compared to these top three factors. Overall, your profile stood out from typical admitted students, who generally show stronger, consistent academic deciles and full-time commitment. This doesn't define your potential, but it highlights areas where the model saw elevated risks based on patterns in past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and exploring full-time options if feasible. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or apply to schools with more flexible profiles, perhaps sharing a personal statement about overcoming early hurdles. With focused improvements, you can reshape how models like this view your candidacy next time.
